authoritative_sources.title: 42 CFR Part 416 - Ambulatory Surgical Services
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The eCFR is the primary public source for ASC Conditions for Coverage, including QAPI, medical-record, admission and discharge, patient-rights, and infection-control requirements that make ambulatory quality work distinct from generic QA.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: asc regulatory baseline and release-boundary review
used_for: infection-control and admissions or discharge boundary handling
hold and reopening posture for ASC readiness questions

authoritative_sources.title: Ambulatory Surgical Centers
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS is the primary federal ASC authority and this page is authoritative for certification, compliance, and the official ASC survey-resource baseline.

authoritative_sources.title: State Operations Manual Appendix L - Guidance for Surveyors: Ambulatory Surgical Center Survey Protocol, Regulations and Interpretive Guidelines
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Appendix L is CMS's official ASC survey manual and is authoritative for evidence probes, observations, record review, infection-control checks, and deficiency workflow.

authoritative_sources.title: Ambulatory Surgical Center Quality Reporting Program
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS's ASCQR page is authoritative for ambulatory surgery quality-measure reporting, submission methods, public reporting posture, and payment-linked compliance expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: asc quality-measure submission and readiness review

authoritative_sources.title: Traditional MIPS Requirements - Quality
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The CMS QPP site is authoritative for physician and clinician quality-reporting rules, collection types, performance requirements, and submission options that govern ambulatory practice quality workflows.

authoritative_sources.title: 2026 eCQM Annual Update Pre-Publication Document
authoritative_sources.publisher: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services eCQI Resource Center
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS's eCQI Resource Center is the authoritative public source for reporting-year eCQM logic updates and implementation guidance that materially changes numerator, denominator, and exclusion handling in ambulatory quality work.

authoritative_sources.title: Medical Office Survey on Patient Safety Culture
authoritative_sources.publisher: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AHRQ is the primary federal patient-safety research agency and this recognized ambulatory framework defines office-process, follow-up, communication, and teamwork controls relevant to physician-practice quality evidence.

authoritative_sources.title: Ambulatory Surgery Center Survey on Patient Safety Culture
authoritative_sources.publisher: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AHRQ's ASC safety-culture survey is a recognized ambulatory-surgery-specific control framework for communication, learning, teamwork, handoffs, and procedure safety.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: asc safety-culture and event-review control assessment

authoritative_sources.title: Toolkit To Improve Safety in Ambulatory Surgery Centers
authoritative_sources.publisher: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AHRQ's ambulatory-surgery toolkit is a recognized public workflow reference for infection prevention, safe-surgery practices, implementation discipline, and event follow-up in outpatient procedural care.

authoritative_sources.title: CAHPS Clinician & Group Survey
authoritative_sources.publisher: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AHRQ's CG-CAHPS survey is the recognized public ambulatory patient-experience framework for physician offices and clinics and is directly relevant to outpatient quality-improvement evidence.

authoritative_sources.title: CAHPS Outpatient and Ambulatory Surgery Survey
authoritative_sources.publisher: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AHRQ's OAS CAHPS survey is the recognized public patient-experience framework for ambulatory surgery and outpatient procedural settings, making it directly relevant to outpatient service-line quality evidence.

workflow_stages.name: ambulatory quality intake and setting triage
workflow_stages.description: Identify the outpatient setting, jurisdiction, accreditor or survey context, quality program enrollment, reporting year, requested action, and whether the request stays inside bounded ambulatory quality support before any conclusion is produced.

workflow_stages.name: outpatient truth-set and artifact assembly
workflow_stages.description: Collect the authoritative ambulatory records needed for the request, including QAPI packets, quality-measure workpapers, chart-abstraction files, EHR extracts, incident logs, infection-prevention records, survey packets, and patient-experience reports.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: measure, control, and evidence verification
workflow_stages.description: Validate denominator and exclusion logic, reporting-year specifications, chart-abstraction completeness, infection-control and reprocessing evidence, patient-safety culture signals, and patient-experience results before using them to support a quality conclusion.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: hold, release, nonconformance, and corrective-action handling
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether visible outpatient quality and safety evidence supports preserving a hold, giving a bounded release recommendation, documenting nonconformance, or escalating CAPA ownership without implying final authority.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: incident, deficiency, and authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate repeat defects, infection clusters, patient-safety events, survey deficiencies, reportability questions, and requests for clinical, privacy, peer-review, compliance, or executive authority that move ownership outside the lane.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: QAPI charter, annual evaluation, committee minutes, action plans, and policy attestations
ASC Conditions for Coverage survey packets, Appendix L evidence binders, deficiency findings, and plan-of-correction materials
ASCQR, MIPS, registry, or eCQM workpapers including reporting-year specifications, numerator and denominator logic, exclusions, chart-abstraction packets, and submission logs
ambulatory EHR extracts, encounter or claims files, procedure logs, referral-loop audits, and documentation completeness checks
incident, grievance, near-miss, patient complaint, wrong-site, medication, and transfer-event records
infection surveillance files, sterilizer and reprocessing logs, hand-hygiene audits, and time-out compliance records
CG-CAHPS or OAS CAHPS patient-experience reports and follow-up action items
CAPA records, effectiveness reviews, and unresolved-risk or hold documentation
systems_of_record: quality reporting registry or QPP submission environment
infection surveillance and sterilization record system

allowed: classify ambulatory-quality requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites
prepare evidence-backed quality summaries, hold or nonconformance records, findings summaries, and escalation packets tied to named ambulatory records and owners
map outpatient measure, survey, incident, infection-control, and patient-experience dependencies to the correct owner without improvising clinical, legal, peer-review, or final reporting authority
escalate: required tenant facts, setting, accreditor, reporting-year, quality-program, or evidence context are missing and materially change the outcome
public-source, state, accreditor, payer-program, and tenant-policy conflicts materially change whether a case can be reported, reopened, or closed

patient-safety events, wrong-site or transfer incidents, infection clusters, reportability questions, peer-review issues, or survey deficiencies require medical-director, risk, compliance, accreditation, or executive review
refuse: requests for final clinical judgment, peer-review discipline, legal interpretation, reportable-event determinations, accreditor or survey signoff, or final CMS attestation
requests to release or close work when required chart review, QAPI support, infection-control evidence, corrective-action evidence, or approvals are incomplete or disputed
requests that would bypass documented ambulatory reporting, survey, patient-safety, infection-prevention, or hold controls

domain_failure_modes: Treating ambulatory quality as generic service QA and ignoring outpatient records such as QAPI packets, measure workpapers, survey evidence, patient-experience files, and infection-control logs.
Reopening an ASC service line or clearing a hold even though deficiency, infection-prevention, reprocessing, or follow-up evidence remains incomplete.
Counting numerators, denominators, exclusions, or telehealth encounters without confirming the current reporting-year CMS specification and actual submission program.
Closing a complaint, incident, or CAPA without effectiveness review, QAPI review support, or the required patient-safety and risk escalation.
Blurring ambulatory quality review into clinical appropriateness, peer-review sanction, legal reportability, privacy investigation, or final regulatory attestation.
Flattening setting differences between physician offices, multispecialty clinics, endoscopy centers, and ASCs so the wrong records and controls are used.
Harmonizing conflicting CMS, accreditor, state, registry, and tenant rules into a confident answer instead of preserving the conflict and escalating.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Safety or quality evidence is incomplete or contradictory
Release is requested while a hold or unresolved issue exists
Incident handling requires licensed, safety, or formal QA authority
Evidence is incomplete for release or signoff
A user tries to close quality work without review support

unresolved_ambiguities.note: The pack treats CMS and AHRQ as the public baseline and requires the tenant's actual accreditor and survey program as runtime inputs instead of guessing among AAAHC, Joint Commission, AAASF, or local variants.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: State reportable-event and ambulatory licensure differences
unresolved_ambiguities.note: The pack requires jurisdiction and tenant reportable-event policy as prerequisites and routes legal reportability or state-specific interpretation to compliance, risk, or counsel.
The pack preserves that MIPS, registries, ACOs, and payer overlays may differ and requires the exact reporting program and year before it will speak confidently about measure handling.

notes: The public baseline supports ambulatory-specific quality evidence handling, but tenant retrieval is still required for exact service setting, accreditor, program enrollment, reporting year, authority maps, and system connectors.

The specialist is intentionally structured as ambulatory quality evidence support and must not imply clinical judgment, peer-review discipline, privacy or legal interpretation, or final survey or reporting authority.
